bytes.topic: MLOps Philosophy — why reproducibility, versioning, and automation matter in ML
bytes.title: wandb MLOps — Reproducibility, Versioning, and Automation Done Right
bytes.body: Most ML bugs aren't model bugs — they're provenance bugs. You can't reproduce a result because you don't know which data version, config, or random seed produced it. wandb fixes this at every layer. `wandb.init(config=cfg)` snapshots your hyperparameters; `wandb.log()` records metrics per step with a wall-clock timestamp. Artifacts version your datasets and models: `wandb.Artifact('dataset', type='dataset')` creates an immutable, content-hashed snapshot you can `use_artifact()` downstream. Every run automatically captures Git commit SHA, diff, and environment — so `wandb.restore()` can reconstruct the exact state. For automation, Sweeps replace manual grid searches: define a YAML search space, call `wandb.agent(sweep_id, train_fn)`, and the controller parallelizes trials across machines. Registry integrations let you promote a versioned artifact to `production` alias via the API, triggering downstream CI checks. The result: every experiment is a first-class, auditable artifact — not a notebook someone ran at 2am.
bytes.code_snippet:
import wandb

with wandb.init(project="churn-model", config={"lr": 1e-3, "epochs": 10}) as run:
    dataset = run.use_artifact("team/churn-model/dataset:v3")
    data_dir = dataset.download()

    for epoch in range(run.config.epochs):
        loss = train_epoch(data_dir, lr=run.config.lr)
        run.log({"train/loss": loss, "epoch": epoch})

    model_artifact = wandb.Artifact("churn-classifier", type="model")
    model_artifact.add_file("model.pt")
    run.log_artifact(model_artifact, aliases=["staging"])

bytes.topic: Experiment Tracking — logging metrics, params, artifacts, comparing runs
bytes.title: wandb Experiment Tracking — Logging, Artifacts, and Run Comparison That Actually Scales
bytes.body:
Every wandb run starts with `wandb.init()`, where `project` and `config` are your first-class citizens. Pass hyperparameters via `config` — not hardcoded — so they're queryable in the UI later. Use `wandb.log()` inside your training loop to stream metrics step-by-step; the `step` parameter lets you align multiple metrics on the same x-axis across runs.

For artifacts, `wandb.Artifact` gives you versioned, content-addressed storage. Log a model checkpoint with `artifact.add_file()` then `run.log_artifact()` — wandb computes a digest so identical files aren't re-uploaded. Tag artifacts with `aliases` like `best` or `production` to create a promotion pipeline without copying files.

Run comparison lives in the Table view: group by config keys (e.g. `learning_rate`, `optimizer`), then plot parallel coordinates to spot which hyperparameter ranges dominate your validation loss. Use `wandb.summary` to pin a single scalar — like best val accuracy — that survives across the full run history and surfaces cleanly in the runs table for sorting.
bytes.code_snippet:
import wandb

run = wandb.init(
    project="image-classifier",
    config={"lr": 1e-3, "epochs": 20, "optimizer": "adamw", "batch_size": 64}
)

for epoch in range(run.config.epochs):
    train_loss = train_one_epoch()
    val_acc = evaluate()
    wandb.log({"train/loss": train_loss, "val/accuracy": val_acc}, step=epoch)

wandb.summary["best_val_accuracy"] = best_val_acc

artifact = wandb.Artifact("model-checkpoint", type="model")
artifact.add_file("checkpoint.pt")
run.log_artifact(artifact, aliases=["best"])

run.finish()

bytes.topic: MLflow — tracking server, model registry, projects, model serving
bytes.title: wandb vs MLflow — Tracking, Registry, Projects, and Serving in the W&B Ecosystem
bytes.body: wandb replicates MLflow's four pillars — tracking, registry, projects, and serving — with deeper automation. For experiment tracking, `wandb.init()` + `wandb.log()` replace MLflow's tracking server; GPU, CPU, and memory metrics are captured automatically without extra instrumentation. For the model registry, log a checkpoint via `wandb.Artifact(type="model")`, then promote it with `run.link_artifact(..., aliases=["production"])` — no separate `MlflowClient()` required. For reproducibility (MLflow Projects equivalent), wandb auto-captures your `config` dict, git SHA, pip requirements, and environment on every run, all queryable via `wandb.Api()`. For serving, wandb Launch deploys registered models to Kubernetes, SageMaker, or Docker using a declarative job config — replacing `mlflow models serve`. The critical UX difference: alias changes (e.g., promoting to `production`) can trigger Slack alerts or webhook automations directly from the registry, enabling CI/CD pipelines without external orchestration.
bytes.code_snippet:
import wandb

# Tracking (replaces MLflow tracking server + start_run)
run = wandb.init(project="recommender", job_type="train", config={"lr": 0.001, "epochs": 20})
wandb.log({"train_loss": 0.38, "val_auc": 0.94})

# Model Registry (replaces mlflow.sklearn.log_model + MlflowClient)
artifact = wandb.Artifact("rec-model", type="model")
artifact.add_file("model.pt")
run.log_artifact(artifact)

# Promote to registry with alias (replaces transition_model_version_stage)
run.link_artifact(artifact, "my-entity/model-registry/rec-model", aliases=["production"])

run.finish()

bytes.topic: Weights & Biases — experiment tracking, sweeps for hyperparameter search, reports
bytes.title: wandb Sweeps — Hyperparameter Search Without the Boilerplate
bytes.body:
wandb Sweeps automate hyperparameter search across your training runs, logging every trial to a shared project so you can compare results in one place. Define a sweep config with a search strategy — `grid`, `random`, or `bayes` — and wandb handles agent scheduling, early termination via `HyperbandEarlyStopper`, and result aggregation automatically.

The Bayesian strategy uses a Gaussian process to model the loss surface, prioritizing promising regions rather than sampling blindly. This converges faster than random search on most deep learning workloads.

Each agent calls your training function, which reads hyperparams via `wandb.config` — no argparse plumbing needed. Parallel agents share the same sweep ID, so you can distribute across GPUs or machines with a single `wandb agent <sweep_id>` command.

After runs complete, use `wandb.Api().sweep(sweep_id).best_run()` programmatically to pull the top checkpoint, or open the Sweep dashboard to view parallel coordinates plots that reveal which params actually drive your metric. Pin the best run to a wandb Report to share findings with your team without exporting a single CSV.
bytes.code_snippet:
import wandb

sweep_config = {
    "method": "bayes",
    "metric": {"name": "val_loss", "goal": "minimize"},
    "early_terminate": {"type": "hyperband", "min_iter": 3},
    "parameters": {
        "lr":        {"distribution": "log_uniform_values", "min": 1e-5, "max": 1e-2},
        "dropout":   {"values": [0.1, 0.3, 0.5]},
        "batch_size":{"values": [32, 64, 128]},
    },
}

def train():
    with wandb.init() as run:
        cfg = run.config
        model = build_model(dropout=cfg.dropout)
        optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=cfg.lr)
        for epoch in range(20):
            loss = run_epoch(model, optimizer, cfg.batch_size)
            wandb.log({"val_loss": loss, "epoch": epoch})

sweep_id = wandb.sweep(sweep_config, project="my-project")
wandb.agent(sweep_id, function=train, count=30)

bytes.topic: DVC — data versioning, pipeline stages, remote storage, reproducible pipelines
bytes.title: wandb + DVC — Wiring Data Versioning Into Your Experiment Runs
bytes.body:
DVC and wandb solve different halves of the reproducibility problem. DVC owns your data files, pipeline stages, and `params.yaml` — wandb owns run history, metric curves, and model artifacts. Wire them together by reading `params.yaml` inside each DVC stage and passing it directly to `wandb.init(config=params)`. Now every `dvc repro` produces a tracked wandb run stamped with the exact hyperparams used.

For remote storage, keep large datasets in your DVC remote (S3/GCS via `dvc push/pull`) and push model checkpoints to wandb Artifacts — the two systems don't overlap. Use `dvc params diff HEAD~1` to see what changed between pipeline runs, then cross-reference against wandb's run comparison panel to see how metrics shifted.

The critical integration point: write DVC stage `outs:` to disk *and* log them via `wandb.log()`. DVC's file-level content hashing gives you exact reproducibility and cache invalidation; wandb's UI gives you cross-run analysis and collaboration. Neither tool alone gives you both. Tag each wandb run with the active DVC commit via `wandb.run.tags` so you can always trace a metric back to the pipeline version that produced it.
bytes.code_snippet:
import json, yaml, wandb

with open("params.yaml") as f:
    params = yaml.safe_load(f)["train"]

run = wandb.init(project="my-model", config=params,
                 tags=["dvc"])
wandb.run.tags += ("dvc-" + open(".dvc/tmp/run-cache").read()[:7],)

model = train(lr=params["lr"], epochs=params["epochs"])
metrics = evaluate(model)

wandb.log(metrics)

with open("metrics/scores.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(metrics, f)

model.save("models/model.pkl")
wandb.save("models/model.pkl")
run.finish()

bytes.topic: Model Registry — versioning models, staging vs production, approval workflows
bytes.title: wandb Model Registry — Staging, Production, and Approval Workflows
bytes.body:
wandb's Model Registry centralizes versioned model artifacts with explicit lifecycle stages. When you log a model via `wandb.log_artifact()`, it lands as a candidate version. From there, you promote it through stages — `staging` and `production` — using the Registry UI or the API.

Each model version is an immutable artifact linked to the run that produced it, giving you full lineage: hyperparams, metrics, dataset version, and code commit. Promotion isn't automatic — Registry supports approval workflows where designated reviewers must sign off before a version reaches `production`. This prevents hot-fix accidents and creates an auditable trail.

Programmatically, use `wandb.Api()` to fetch a registered model and update its aliases. Aliases like `staging` and `production` are mutable pointers; the underlying artifact version never changes. Downstream systems (CI pipelines, inference servers) pin to an alias, so promoting is a one-line alias reassignment — no redeployment of config files needed. Combine this with webhook triggers on stage transitions to automate downstream deployment.
bytes.code_snippet:
import wandb

api = wandb.Api()

# Link a logged artifact to the Model Registry
run = wandb.init(project="fraud-detection")
artifact = run.use_artifact("fraud-model:v12", type="model")

# Promote to staging (run this after validation)
registered_model = api.artifact("team/fraud-detection/fraud-model:v12")
registered_model.aliases.append("staging")
registered_model.save()

# After approval workflow completes, promote to production
registered_model.aliases = [a for a in registered_model.aliases if a != "staging"]
registered_model.aliases.append("production")
registered_model.save()

wandb.finish()

bytes.topic: ONNX — cross-framework model format, export process, runtime inference
bytes.title: wandb Artifacts — Versioning ONNX Exports and Tracking Inference Benchmarks
bytes.body: ONNX gives you a framework-agnostic snapshot of your model — but without provenance, that .onnx file is just a black box. wandb closes that gap. After exporting via torch.onnx.export(), log the file as a wandb.Artifact with type='model' and attach the originating run's config as metadata. This links the ONNX graph back to the exact hyperparameters, dataset version, and training run that produced it. From there, run onnxruntime.InferenceSession on the exported file and log latency and throughput with wandb.log() alongside the original PyTorch baseline — now you have a side-by-side latency comparison tracked across every export. Tag the artifact as 'production' via the wandb Model Registry when it passes your latency threshold. Downstream consumers (serving, mobile pipelines) can pull the artifact by alias rather than filename, so promotions are atomic and auditable. Use wandb.use_artifact() in your inference benchmark script to always test the latest registered version without hardcoding paths.
bytes.code_snippet:
import wandb
import torch
import onnxruntime as ort
import numpy as np
import time

run = wandb.init(project="onnx-export", job_type="export")

torch.onnx.export(model, dummy_input, "model.onnx", opset_version=17,
                  input_names=["input"], output_names=["output"],
                  dynamic_axes={"input": {0: "batch"}})

artifact = wandb.Artifact("resnet50-onnx", type="model",
                          metadata={"opset": 17, "framework": "pytorch",
                                    "config": dict(run.config)})
artifact.add_file("model.onnx")
run.log_artifact(artifact)

sess = ort.InferenceSession("model.onnx", providers=["CPUExecutionProvider"])
start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(100):
    sess.run(None, {"input": np.random.randn(1, 3, 224, 224).astype(np.float32)})
wandb.log({"onnx_latency_ms": (time.perf_counter() - start) / 100 * 1000})
run.finish()

bytes.topic: TensorRT — NVIDIA optimization, quantization, engine building, latency vs accuracy
bytes.title: wandb + TensorRT — Tracking Quantization Tradeoffs Across Engine Builds
bytes.body: When building TensorRT engines, INT8 calibration can slash latency by 2–4x but silently erode accuracy. wandb makes this tradeoff visible across every engine variant you ship. Log engine build metadata — precision mode, workspace size, calibration dataset hash — as `wandb.config`, then benchmark each engine and push latency percentiles and task-specific accuracy (mAP, top-1, BLEU) as `wandb.log` metrics. Use `wandb.log({"p50_latency_ms": ..., "p99_latency_ms": ..., "accuracy": ...})` per engine run so the Summary table lets you sort by the Pareto frontier: best accuracy at a given latency budget. Tag runs with `tags=["fp32", "int8", "fp16"]` to filter instantly in the workspace. Store the serialized `.engine` file as a `wandb.Artifact` with `type="trt-engine"` — this pins the exact binary to its benchmark result, so you can `use_artifact()` to redeploy the winning engine without rebuilding. Use `wandb.Table` to log per-layer precision assignments when debugging accuracy regressions from forced INT8 layers.
bytes.code_snippet:
import wandb
import tensorrt as trt

run = wandb.init(project="trt-optimization", config={
    "precision": "int8",
    "workspace_gb": 4,
    "calib_batches": 100,
})

# Build engine (assume build_engine() returns (engine, build_time_ms))
engine, build_ms = build_engine(config=wandb.config)

# Benchmark
p50, p99, accuracy = benchmark_engine(engine)
wandb.log({"p50_latency_ms": p50, "p99_latency_ms": p99, "accuracy": accuracy, "build_time_ms": build_ms})

# Persist engine binary as artifact
artifact = wandb.Artifact("resnet50-trt", type="trt-engine")
artifact.add_file("model.engine")
run.log_artifact(artifact)

run.finish()

bytes.topic: Ray — distributed training, Ray Tune for HPO, Ray Serve for model serving
bytes.title: wandb + Ray Tune — Logging Every HPO Trial Automatically
bytes.body:
When running distributed hyperparameter search with Ray Tune, `WandbLoggerCallback` maps each trial to a separate wandb run inside a shared project — no manual `wandb.init()` per worker needed. Pass it directly to `tune.run()` or `Tuner` and every trial's config, metrics, and system stats stream to wandb in real time.

For distributed training with Ray Train, use the `WandbLoggerCallback` from `ray.air.integrations.wandb`. It initializes wandb on the rank-0 worker only, preventing duplicate metric writes across nodes.

To group a full HPO sweep for comparison, set `group` in the callback's `wandb_init_kwargs`. This surfaces all trials under one sweep-style view in the wandb UI — filter by `trial_id` to isolate any run.

For Ray Serve deployments, instrument your `__call__` handler with `wandb.log()` to track live inference latency and request volume. Use `wandb.init(reinit=False)` at the deployment class level so the run persists across requests without reinitializing on every call.

bytes.topic: Hyperparameter Optimization — grid vs random vs Bayesian search, early stopping
bytes.title: wandb Sweeps — Grid vs Random vs Bayesian Search and Early Stopping
bytes.body:
wandb Sweeps gives you three search strategies via `method` in your sweep config. `grid` exhaustively tries every parameter combination — only viable with small, discrete search spaces. `random` samples combinations independently; it outperforms grid when some parameters matter far more than others. `bayes` uses a Gaussian Process to model the objective surface, selecting next runs by expected improvement — the right default for most deep learning sweeps with continuous parameters.

Early stopping is controlled by the `early_terminate` block. `hyperband` is the standard choice: set `min_iter` to the minimum epochs before termination is considered, and `eta` (default 3) as the bracket reduction factor. Runs in the bottom 1/eta bracket are killed at each rung.

Initiate a sweep with `wandb.sweep(config, project='my-project')` — this returns a `sweep_id`. Launch agents with `wandb.agent(sweep_id, function=train, count=50)`. Each agent calls `wandb.config` to read its assigned hyperparameters, and `wandb.log({'val_loss': loss})` to report the metric Sweeps optimizes against.

bytes.topic: Model Serving — REST APIs for inference, batching, latency SLAs
bytes.title: wandb Model Registry to REST API — Artifact Aliases, Latency Logging, and SLA Alerts
bytes.body:
Deploying a model registered in W&B's Model Registry as a REST endpoint ties your serving layer directly to an artifact alias — `production`, `staging`, or a semantic version tag. At server startup, pull the artifact with `wandb.Api().artifact("entity/project/model:production")` and call `.download()` to materialize weights locally. Pair this with a single long-lived `wandb.init(job_type="serving", resume="allow")` run to stream per-request metrics without the overhead of spawning a new run per call.

For batching, accumulate requests over a configurable window (e.g., 20 ms) before calling `model.predict()`, then log batch size alongside latency: `run.log({"latency_ms": ..., "batch_size": ...})`. SLA enforcement is built into W&B via `run.alert()` — fire a Slack or email notification directly from your serving process when p99 crosses a threshold using `wandb.AlertLevel.WARN`. Crucially, pinning the artifact alias in your deployment config means promoting a new model to production is a single Registry update — no redeployment required if you resolve the alias at startup.
bytes.code_snippet:
import wandb, time
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()
api = wandb.Api()
artifact = api.artifact("myorg/myproject/classifier:production")
model = load_model(artifact.download())  # your loader
run = wandb.init(project="myproject", job_type="serving", resume="allow")

@app.post("/predict")
async def predict(payload: dict):
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    result = model.predict([payload["input"]])[0]
    latency_ms = (time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000
    run.log({"latency_ms": latency_ms})
    if latency_ms > 80:
        run.alert("SLA breach", text=f"{latency_ms:.1f}ms > 80ms p99", level=wandb.AlertLevel.WARN)
    return {"prediction": result}

bytes.topic: Model Monitoring — prediction drift, data drift, performance degradation in prod
bytes.title: wandb — Detecting Model Drift and Performance Degradation in Production
bytes.body: Production models degrade silently. wandb gives you the primitives to catch it early. Log prediction distributions alongside ground truth using `wandb.log()` at inference time — track metrics like PSI (Population Stability Index) or KL divergence on your output logits across rolling windows. For data drift, log feature statistics per batch and use wandb Tables to diff input distributions between training and serving. Set up `wandb.alert()` to fire when a tracked metric crosses a threshold — e.g., when rolling accuracy drops 5% below your baseline run. Use `wandb.run.summary` to pin your prod baseline metrics, then compare live runs against it programmatically via the wandb API (`api.run(path).summary`). For systematic monitoring, create a dedicated wandb Project per environment (train, staging, prod) and use run tags like `env:prod` to filter dashboards. Pair with W&B Reports to build a drift detection dashboard that auto-refreshes — no separate monitoring stack needed. The key is instrumenting your inference service as a long-lived wandb run, not just training.

bytes.topic: CI/CD for ML — automated retraining, evaluation gates, deployment pipelines
bytes.title: wandb Model Registry — Automating CI/CD Evaluation Gates for ML Pipelines
bytes.body:
wandb's Model Registry is the missing piece in ML CI/CD — it lets you version, gate, and promote models via alias rather than hardcoded paths. The pattern: every retraining job logs a `wandb.Artifact` of type `"model"` and stores eval metrics in `artifact.metadata`. Your gate script then fetches the `"production"` alias via `wandb.Api()`, compares metrics, and promotes only if improvement clears the threshold.

Store baseline metrics directly on the artifact at log time: `artifact.metadata = {"eval_accuracy": val}` before `run.log_artifact(artifact)`. This keeps the metric co-located with the model version — no cross-referencing run IDs.

Promotion is a one-liner alias mutation: `artifact.aliases.append("production"); artifact.save()`. Your serving infra polls the `"production"` alias, so it auto-picks up the new version without a redeployment.

Tag training runs with pipeline stage (`ci-retrain`, `ci-eval`) using `run.tags`, then filter via `api.runs(filters={"tags": ["ci-retrain"]})` for full lineage. Pair with `wandb.alert()` inside the eval run to fire Slack notifications on gate failures — keeping humans in the loop only when automation breaks down.
bytes.code_snippet:
import wandb

api = wandb.Api()
THRESHOLD = 0.005  # require 0.5% improvement

# Pull production baseline metric from registry artifact
prod = api.artifact("myorg/myproject/model:production")
baseline_acc = prod.metadata["eval_accuracy"]

# Get latest CI retraining run
runs = api.runs("myorg/myproject", filters={"tags": ["ci-retrain"]}, order="-created_at")
new_acc = runs[0].summary.get("eval/accuracy", 0.0)

# Evaluation gate: promote only on meaningful gain
if new_acc > baseline_acc + THRESHOLD:
    candidate = api.artifact("myorg/myproject/model:latest")
    candidate.aliases.append("production")
    candidate.save()
    print(f"Promoted: {new_acc:.4f} vs baseline {baseline_acc:.4f}")
else:
    print(f"Gate failed: {new_acc:.4f} did not clear {baseline_acc + THRESHOLD:.4f}")

bytes.topic: Feature Stores — centralized feature management, online vs offline features
bytes.title: wandb Artifacts as a Feature Store — Versioning Offline Features with Full Lineage
bytes.body:
Feature stores split features into offline (batch, historical, used for training) and online (low-latency, used at inference). wandb Artifacts cover the offline side natively — version your feature DataFrames as `type="dataset"` artifacts using `wandb.Table`, and every training run records exactly which feature snapshot it consumed via `run.use_artifact()`. Attach metadata (row counts, column list, pipeline git hash) directly to the artifact so the W&B Registry becomes queryable for drift audits or snapshot rollbacks.

For online features, wandb doesn't run a low-latency key-value store — pair it with Redis or Feast for that layer — but log the feature schema and summary statistics to the same artifact so offline and online definitions stay in sync.

The canonical wandb pattern: a dedicated `job_type="feature-engineering"` run produces and logs the artifact; all downstream `job_type="train"` runs call `run.use_artifact("user-features:latest")`. The full producer-consumer DAG appears in the Artifacts lineage graph, giving you an audit trail from raw data to deployed model checkpoint without a separate feature registry.
bytes.code_snippet:
import wandb, pandas as pd

# Feature engineering — produce offline features
run = wandb.init(project="mlops-demo", job_type="feature-engineering")
artifact = wandb.Artifact("user-features", type="dataset",
                          metadata={"features": ["clicks", "dwell_time", "recency"]})
artifact.add(wandb.Table(dataframe=compute_features()), "offline")
run.log_artifact(artifact)
run.finish()

# Training — consume exact versioned snapshot
run = wandb.init(project="mlops-demo", job_type="train")
table = run.use_artifact("user-features:latest").get("offline")
X = pd.DataFrame(data=table.data, columns=table.columns)

bytes.topic: Quantization & Pruning — reducing model size, int8/fp16, trade-offs
bytes.title: wandb — Tracking Quantization & Pruning Trade-offs Without Losing Your Mind
bytes.body:
Quantization and pruning degrade models in ways that aggregate metrics hide. A model quantized to int8 might hold 98% accuracy on your test set but collapse on tail distributions. wandb's artifact versioning and custom charts let you surface these failures systematically.

Log your baseline run with `wandb.log({"model_size_mb": ..., "latency_ms": ..., "accuracy": ...})`, then after applying `torch.quantization.quantize_dynamic` or structured pruning via `torch.nn.utils.prune`, create a new artifact version: `wandb.log_artifact(artifact, aliases=["int8"])`. Use `wandb.use_artifact("model:baseline")` vs `"model:int8"` to diff directly in the UI.

For per-layer sensitivity analysis, log a table with `wandb.log({"layer_sensitivity": wandb.Table(data=rows, columns=["layer", "sparsity", "accuracy_drop"])})`. This reveals which layers tolerate aggressive pruning (early conv layers usually) vs which are brittle (attention heads, final classifiers).

Parallel coordinates plots in wandb let you sweep `sparsity_ratio` and `quantization_dtype` simultaneously — critical for finding the Pareto front between model size and accuracy before committing to a compression strategy.
bytes.code_snippet:
import wandb
import torch
import torch.nn.utils.prune as prune

with wandb.init(project="model-compression", config={"sparsity": 0.4, "dtype": "int8"}) as run:
    model = load_model()
    
    # Structured pruning on conv layers
    for name, module in model.named_modules():
        if isinstance(module, torch.nn.Conv2d):
            prune.l1_unstructured(module, name="weight", amount=run.config.sparsity)
    
    # Log compression metrics
    run.log({
        "model_size_mb": get_model_size(model),
        "latency_ms": benchmark_latency(model),
        "accuracy": evaluate(model),
        "layer_sensitivity": wandb.Table(
            data=[[n, run.config.sparsity, delta_acc(model, n)] for n in pruned_layers],
            columns=["layer", "sparsity", "accuracy_drop"]
        )
    })
    
    artifact = wandb.Artifact("compressed-model", type="model")
    artifact.add_file("model_pruned.pt")
    run.log_artifact(artifact, aliases=[f"sparsity-{run.config.sparsity}"])

bytes.topic: A/B Testing Models — shadow deployment, canary releases, champion/challenger
bytes.title: wandb A/B Testing Models — Shadow Deployments, Canary Releases, and Champion/Challenger
bytes.body:
wandb's Model Registry makes champion/challenger workflows first-class. Tag your production model as `champion` and candidates as `challenger` using `run.link_model()` with aliases. For shadow deployments, log both models' predictions to the same run using separate namespaces — `wandb.log({"champion/latency": ..., "challenger/latency": ...})` — so you get side-by-side metric charts without serving challenger traffic to real users.

For canary releases, use `wandb.Api()` to programmatically fetch the challenger artifact, route a percentage of traffic to it, and log outcomes back to a shared project. Compare challenger vs champion using `wandb.plot.line_series()` on a shared dashboard panel.

Promote the winner with `artifact.aliases.append('champion')` and remove the old alias — Registry tracks the full lineage. Use `wandb.alert()` to trigger Slack notifications if challenger diverges on key metrics like F1 or p95 latency. The entire experiment history, including data splits and hyperparameters, stays linked via the artifact graph.
bytes.code_snippet:
import wandb

api = wandb.Api()

# Fetch champion and challenger from Model Registry
champion = api.artifact("my-org/model-registry/classifier:champion")
challenger = api.artifact("my-org/model-registry/classifier:challenger")

with wandb.init(project="ab-test", job_type="shadow-eval") as run:
    run.use_artifact(champion)
    run.use_artifact(challenger)

    for batch in eval_batches:
        champ_pred = champion_model.predict(batch)
        chal_pred = challenger_model.predict(batch)
        run.log({
            "champion/f1": f1(champ_pred, batch.labels),
            "challenger/f1": f1(chal_pred, batch.labels),
        })

    # Promote if challenger wins
    if challenger_f1 > champion_f1:
        challenger.aliases.append("champion")
        challenger.save()
        wandb.alert(title="New Champion Promoted", text=f"challenger f1={challenger_f1:.3f}")

bytes.topic: Data Pipelines — feature engineering pipelines, data validation, drift detection
bytes.title: wandb Artifacts — Feature Pipeline Lineage, Validation, and Drift Detection
bytes.body:
wandb Artifacts give feature engineering pipelines first-class lineage tracking. Each stage — raw ingestion, transformation, validation — can produce a versioned Artifact, so you always know which feature set trained which model.

For data validation, log per-feature statistics (mean, std, null rate, quantiles) via `wandb.log()` at pipeline completion. Comparing these across artifact versions surfaces distribution shifts before they reach training. Use `wandb.Table` to log per-sample validation results — this makes individual outlier rows queryable directly in the W&B UI.

Drift detection is explicit but effective: pull two artifact versions with `run.use_artifact('features:v1')` and `run.use_artifact('features:v2')`, compute KS or PSI statistics, and log them under a dedicated `drift-monitor` project. Trigger `wandb.alert()` when thresholds breach — a KS stat above 0.1 on a critical feature fires an immediate Slack or email notification.

The result is a full audit trail — artifact → feature statistics → drift score → alert — all linked through W&B's run graph and queryable via the Artifacts lineage view.
bytes.code_snippet:
import wandb
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import ks_2samp

run = wandb.init(project="drift-monitor")

prev = run.use_artifact("features:v1").get("feature_table")
curr = run.use_artifact("features:v2").get("feature_table")

prev_vals = np.array(prev.get_column("user_activity_score"))
curr_vals = np.array(curr.get_column("user_activity_score"))

stat, p_value = ks_2samp(prev_vals, curr_vals)
wandb.log({"ks_stat": stat, "p_value": p_value})

if stat > 0.1:
    wandb.alert(title="Feature Drift Detected", text=f"KS={stat:.3f} on user_activity_score")

run.finish()

bytes.topic: Infrastructure — GPU cluster management, spot instances for training, cost optimization
bytes.title: wandb — Surviving Spot Instance Preemptions Without Losing Training Progress
bytes.body:
Spot instances can cut GPU costs 60–90%, but preemptions kill runs mid-epoch. wandb's resume mechanism makes them viable. Initialize every run with `resume="allow"` and a deterministic `id` derived from your config hash — wandb will reconnect the same run timeline on restart rather than creating orphaned duplicates.

Checkpoint aggressively: save model state to `wandb.run.dir` so it's synced to the artifact store before the instance dies. On restart, pull the latest checkpoint via `wandb.restore()` using the run ID, load it into your model, and continue logging — wandb stitches the step history seamlessly.

For sweep agents on spot clusters, use `wandb agent --count 1` per instance. Each agent picks one sweep trial, completes or crashes, and the sweep scheduler reassigns crashed runs automatically. Pair this with `wandb.log({"gpu_cost_usd": hours * spot_price})` to track real dollar spend per experiment — surfaced directly in the sweep comparison table for cost-per-accuracy tradeoffs.
bytes.code_snippet:
import wandb
import hashlib, json

def make_run_id(config: dict) -> str:
    return hashlib.md5(json.dumps(config, sort_keys=True).encode()).hexdigest()[:8]

config = {"lr": 1e-3, "epochs": 50, "model": "resnet50"}

run = wandb.init(
    project="spot-training",
    id=make_run_id(config),
    resume="allow",
    config=config,
)

# Restore latest checkpoint if resuming
if run.resumed:
    ckpt_file = wandb.restore("checkpoint.pt", run_path=run.path)
    model.load_state_dict(torch.load(ckpt_file.name))

for epoch in range(start_epoch, config["epochs"]):
    loss = train_epoch(model, loader)
    torch.save(model.state_dict(), f"{wandb.run.dir}/checkpoint.pt")
    wandb.save("checkpoint.pt")  # sync immediately
    wandb.log({"loss": loss, "epoch": epoch, "gpu_cost_usd": epoch * SPOT_RATE})
